1: wayyaʿan ʾiyyôḇ ʾeṯ-YHWH wayyōʾmar: Standard dialogue formula — 'Then Job answered YHWH and said.' But the divine name here is the full covenant name YHWH (the tetragrammaton), not the generic ʾĒl or ʾělōhîm that dominates the poetic dialogues. This shift back to YHWH marks the return to the prose-frame register and signals that the dialogue has reached its covenant resolution. Job is answering — not arguing with — the God who has just spoken from the whirlwind for two chapters. The very form of the speech is response, not counter-petition: Job has received his theophany.
2: yāḏaʿtî kî-ḵōl tûḵāl wəlōʾ-yibbāṣēr mimməḵā məzimmāh: 'I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.' Job's first word is yāḏaʿtî (I know, I have come to know — perfect tense, indicating a new state of knowing established by the theophany). The content of his knowledge is divine omnipotence and purposefulness: kōl tûḵāl (you can do all things — yāḵōl with 'all') and lōʾ-yibbāṣēr məzimmāh (no purpose/plan can be cut off from you — bāṣar, to cut off/withhold, is used of withholding grape clusters from harvest). The response engages directly with what YHWH's whirlwind speeches were demonstrating: the Creator-God's comprehensive sovereignty over every created system. Job's theology has been corrected not by argument but by encounter.
3: mî zeh maʿlîm ʿēṣāh bəḇəlî-ḏaʿaṯ lāḵēn higgaḏtî wəlōʾ ʾāḇîn nip̄lāʾôṯ mimmennî wəlōʾ ʾēḏāʿ: 'Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge? Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.' Job quotes YHWH's own challenge from 38:2 back to himself: YHWH had asked 'Who is this that darkens counsel with words without knowledge?' — Job confesses that he is that one. The verb higgaḏtî (I declared, I uttered — from nāḡaḏ, to tell, report, make plain) plus the admission wəlōʾ ʾāḇîn (and I did not understand) is the confession of the limits of creaturely perspective. Nip̄lāʾôṯ (wonders, marvelous things — the same vocabulary as 5:9, 9:10) — Job had spoken about them without knowing them. The confession is not retraction of innocence but acknowledgment of epistemic limitation.
4: šəmaʿ-nāʾ wəʾānōḵî ʾăḏabbēr ʾešʾālăḵā wəhôḏiʿēnî: 'Hear, and I will speak; I will question you, and you make it known to me.' Job quotes YHWH's challenge from 38:3 (and 40:7): 'Dress for action like a man; I will question you, and you make it known to me.' The reversal is striking — Job turns YHWH's challenge back as an invitation: Job has been heard by the whirlwind; now Job echoes the same address. This is not defiance but participation: Job has taken YHWH's challenge seriously, he has 'dressed for action' as requested, and he is now positioned as a genuine dialogue partner. The forensic language of the dialogues reaches its resolution not in a verdict but in an encounter that transforms the plaintiff.
5: ləšēmaʿ-ʾōzen šəmaʿtîḵā wəʿattāh ʿênî rāʾăṯāḵā: 'I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you.' The epistemological pivot of the book. Lišmôaʿ ʾōzen šəmaʿtîḵā (by the hearing of the ear I had heard you) vs. ʿênî rāʾăṯāḵā (my eye has seen you) — the contrast of secondhand report (šēmaʿ) and direct vision (rāʾāh) is the fundamental distinction between inherited theology and experiential encounter. Job has been a man of inherited wisdom throughout; the dialogues have progressively stripped that away until only the raw encounter remains. The 'seeing' is not visual but the total transformative experience of divine presence. This verse is the book's confessional climax: all the theodicy arguments, all the speeches, all the suffering — they produced this transformation. The NT equivalent is the movement from faith based on testimony to faith based on encounter (John 20:29: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' — which presupposes that seeing produces a qualitatively different faith).
6: ʿal-kēn ʾemʾas wəniḥamtî ʿal-ʿāp̄ār wāʾēp̄er: 'Therefore I despise myself and repent in dust and ashes.' One of the book's most debated verses. The key interpretive question: what is the object of māʾas (to despise, reject, loathe)? There is no explicit object in the Hebrew: ʾemʾas is simply 'I despise/I reject' — the object must be supplied. Options: (1) 'I despise myself' (most traditional translations); (2) 'I retract [my words/case]' (treating the lawsuit as dropped); (3) 'I am comforted over dust and ashes' (taking wəniḥamtî as main verb, ʿal as 'over', and ʿāp̄ār wāʾēp̄er as the condition of mourning Job is leaving behind). The third reading has commended itself to many modern scholars: Job is not self-condemning (which would retroactively validate the friends' theology that YHWH condemned in v.7) but rather being consoled and leaving the state of dust-and-ashes mourning that began at 2:8. Whatever the exact reading, the verse marks the resolution of Job's suffering-posture: he is no longer at war with God but at peace.
7: wayhî ʾaḥar diyyabber YHWH ʾeṯ-haddəḇārîm hāʾēlleh ʾel-ʾiyyôḇ wayyōʾmer YHWH ʾel-ʾělîp̄az hattêmānî ḥārāh ʾap̄pî ḇəḵā ûḇišnê rēʿeḵā kî lōʾ dibbarttem ʾēlay nəḵônāh kaʿaḇdî ʾiyyôḇ: 'After YHWH had spoken these words to Job, YHWH said to Eliphaz the Temanite: "My anger burns against you and against your two friends, for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has."' This is YHWH's decisive theological verdict. The divine ḥārāh ʾap̄ (burning anger) — the strongest biblical expression of divine wrath — is directed against the three friends. The grounds: lōʾ dibbarttem ʾēlay nəḵônāh (you have not spoken of me what is right/established). The contrast with Job: kaʿaḇdî ʾiyyôḇ (as my servant Job). The fourfold repetition of ʿaḇdî ʾiyyôḇ (my servant Job) in vv.7-8 restores the prologue's characterization (1:8; 2:3: 'Have you considered my servant Job?'). Despite all of Job's complaints, his raw protest was more theologically true than the friends' polished theodicy. The criterion for 'speaking rightly' about God is not formal theological correctness but honest engagement with experienced reality before a personal God.
8: wəʿattāh qəḥû-lākem šiḇʿāh pārîm wəšiḇʿāh ʾêlîm wəlăḵû ʾel-ʿaḇdî ʾiyyôḇ wəhaʿălîṯem ʿôlāh ḇaʿăḏkem wəʿaḇdî ʾiyyôḇ yiṯpallēl ʿălêkem kî ʾim-pānāyw ʾeśśāʾ ləḇiltî ʿăśôṯ ʿimmākem nəḇālāh kî lōʾ dibbarttem ʾēlay nəḵônāh kaʿaḇdî ʾiyyôḇ: 'Now therefore take seven bulls and seven rams and go to my servant Job and offer up a burnt offering for yourselves. And my servant Job shall pray for you, for I will accept his prayer not to deal with you according to your folly. For you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.' The sevenfold burnt offering (the same as the prologue's offerings, 1:5) requires intercessory prayer by the very man the friends had falsely accused. This inversion is total: Job who needed vindication becomes the intercessor who provides it; the friends who claimed theological authority must humble themselves before the suffering man they lectured. Nəḇālāh (folly, the same word as for the wicked folly of Nabal and the shameful acts of the covenant — Deut 22:21; 2 Sam 13:12) characterizes the friends' speeches as covenant-violation, not merely theological error. Job's intercession — the act of a priest for the guilty — is simultaneously his vindication and their salvation.
9: wayyeləḵû ʾělîp̄az hattêmānî wəḇilḏaḏ haššûḥî ṣôp̄ar hannāʿămaṯî wayyaʿăśû kaʾăšer dibbēr ʾălêhem YHWH wayyiśśāʾ YHWH ʾeṯ-pənê ʾiyyôḇ: 'So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Zophar the Naamathite went and did what YHWH had told them, and YHWH accepted Job's prayer.' Wayyiśśāʾ YHWH ʾeṯ-pənê ʾiyyôḇ (YHWH lifted up the face of Job) is the idiom for favorable acceptance: to 'lift the face' is to receive someone favorably, show them grace (cf. Gen 32:20; 2 Sam 2:22; Mal 1:9). YHWH's lifting of Job's face is the reversal of every 'hiding of the divine face' that Job feared throughout the dialogues. The friends' compliance with YHWH's command — offering the sacrifice, receiving Job's intercession — is their act of faith and the resolution of the theological dispute in YHWH's favor rather than theirs.
10: waYHWH šāḇ ʾeṯ-šəḇîṯ ʾiyyôḇ bəhiṯpallělô bəʿaḏ rēʿēhû wayyōsep̄ YHWH ʾeṯ-ḵol-ʾăšer-lěʾiyyôḇ ləmiš̆neh: 'And YHWH restored the fortunes of Job when he had prayed for his friends. And YHWH gave Job twice as much as he had before.' The phrase šāḇ ʾeṯ-šəḇîṯ (restored the fortunes, turned the captivity) is the standard idiom for divine restoration after judgment (Deut 30:3; Jer 29:14; 30:3; Ezek 16:53; Ps 14:7; 85:1). Crucially, the restoration comes bəhiṯpallělô bəʿaḏ rēʿēhû (when he prayed for his friends) — intercession precedes restoration. The doubling (miš̆neh, twice as much) fulfills the cosmic test: Job who lost everything for his integrity now receives double, not as a reward for correct theology but as the vindication of faithful suffering. The NT pattern: Christ who interceded for enemies ('Father, forgive them') and suffered in faithfulness receives the inheritance that includes redeemed enemies.
11: wayyāḇōʾû ʾēlāyw ḵol-ʾeḥāyw wəḵol-ʾaḥyōṯāyw wəḵol-yōḏəʿāyw ləp̄ānîm wayyōʾḵəlû ʿimmô leḥem bəḇêṯô wayyānūḏû lô waynāḥăm lô ʿal-ḵol-hārāʿāh ʾăšer-hēḇîʾ YHWH ʿālāyw wayyittənû-lô ʾîš qəśîṭāh ʾeḥāṯ wəʾîš nèzem zāhāḇ ʾeḥāḏ: 'Then came to him all his brothers and sisters and all who had known him before, and ate bread with him in his house. And they showed him sympathy and comforted him for all the evil that YHWH had brought upon him. And each of them gave him a piece of money and a ring of gold.' The restoration of community (ʾeḥāyw... ʾaḥyōṯāyw... yōḏəʿāyw) reverses the isolation of the suffering period. The admission that the evil came from YHWH (hārāʿāh ʾăšer-hēḇîʾ YHWH ʿālāyw) is remarkable — the prose frame does not soften the divine causation of Job's suffering even in the restoration. Qəśîṭāh (a piece of silver/money, an ancient weight-unit appearing in Gen 33:19 and Josh 24:32) and gold rings are the tokens of restored honor. The covenant community's restoration is essential to the complete restoration: wealth without community is not shalom.
12: waYHWH bēraḵ ʾeṯ-ʾaḥărîṯ ʾiyyôḇ mērēʾšiṯô wayhî-lô ʾarbaʿāʿāśār ʾělep̄ ṣōʾn wəšēšeṯ ʾălāp̄îm gəmallîm wəʾălep̄-ṣeméḏ-ḇāqār wəʾălep̄ ʾăṯônôṯ: 'And YHWH blessed the latter days of Job more than his beginning. And he had 14,000 sheep, 6,000 camels, 1,000 yoke of oxen, and 1,000 female donkeys.' The doubling is mathematically precise: the prologue reported 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen, 500 donkeys (1:3) — all exactly doubled. Bēraḵ... ʾaḥărîṯ... mērēʾšiṯô (blessed the latter days more than the beginning) fulfills Eliphaz's promised restoration (5:17-26) but through a mechanism Eliphaz could not have predicted: not through formulaic repentance but through faithful suffering and intercession. The ʾaḥărîṯ (end, latter days) echoes the wisdom literature's emphasis on the ʾaḥărîṯ of the righteous as more blessed than their beginning (Prov 4:18; 23:18; 24:14).
13: wayhî-lô šiḇʿānāh bānîm wəšālôš bānôṯ: 'He had also seven sons and three daughters.' The children are the same number as the prologue's (1:2: seven sons and three daughters). The livestock was doubled; the children are the same count. The contrast is pointed: the cattle were straightforwardly doubled, but children cannot be doubled in the same way — the ten children of the prologue are not replaced by twenty. The same ten children are restored: the prologue's children still live, in the hope of resurrection that 19:25-27 already anticipated. The NT theological claim: the resurrection is not replacement but restoration. Job does not get ten new children to make up for ten dead ones; he gets the same ten back (in the scope of the promise), along with ten more in his restored life.
14: wayyiqrāʾ šēm-hāʾaḥaṯ yəmîmāh wəšēm haššēnîṯ qəṣîʿāh wəšēm haššəlîšîṯ qeren happûḵ: 'And he called the name of the first daughter Jemimah, and the name of the second Keziah, and the name of the third Keren-happuch.' The daughters are named; the sons are not. This is deliberate: in the ancient Near East, sons were named because they carried the family line; daughters were typically unnamed in genealogies. Job's naming of all three daughters — and the subsequent note about their inheritance (v.15) — is a counter-cultural act of honor. The names: Yəmîmāh (Jemimah, turtle-dove or day — beauty), Qəṣîʿāh (Keziah, cassia bark — fragrance), Qeren happûḵ (Keren-happuch, horn of eyeshadow/antimony — beauty treatment, dazzling eyes). All three are names of beauty and fragrance — Job's daughters are celebrated for their appearance (v.15: no women so beautiful in all the land).
15: wəlōʾ nimṣāʾ nāšîm yəp̄ôṯ kîḇənôṯ ʾiyyôḇ bəḵol-hāʾāreṣ wayyittēn lāhem ʾăḇîhem naḥălāh bəṯôḵ ʾăḥêhem: 'And in all the land there were no women so beautiful as Job's daughters. And their father gave them an inheritance among their brothers.' Two exceptional honors. First, lōʾ nimṣāʾ nāšîm yəp̄ôṯ (no women so beautiful found) throughout the land — the beauty formula that appears for Sarah (implicitly, Gen 12:11), Rebekah (24:16), Rachel (29:17), and Abigail (1 Sam 25:3). Second, and more radical: wayyittēn lāhem ʾăḇîhem naḥălāh bəṯôḵ ʾăḥêhem (their father gave them inheritance among their brothers). Daughters inherited only when there were no sons (Num 27:8, the daughters of Zelophehad). Job's deliberate granting of inheritance to daughters-alongside-sons is a restorative act that exceeds legal obligation — an anticipation of the new covenant's abolition of inheritance distinctions (Gal 3:28-29: neither male nor female, all heirs according to the promise).
16: wayhî ʾiyyôḇ ʾaḥar-zōʾṯ mēʾāh wəʾarbaʿîm šānāh wayyarə ʾeṯ-bānāyw wəʾeṯ-bənê ḇānāyw ʾarbaʿāh ḏōrôṯ: 'And after this Job lived 140 years, and saw his sons, and his sons' sons, four generations.' The lifespan of 140 years after the restoration corresponds to the patriarchal pattern: Isaac lived 180 years (Gen 35:28), Jacob 147 years (Gen 47:28), Joseph 110 years (Gen 50:26). The fourfold generation ('sons, sons' sons, four generations') is the fullest possible vision of covenant blessing: to see one's great-great-grandchildren is the ancient world's image of completed fulfillment. Eliphaz's promise (5:25: 'your offspring shall be many, and your descendants as the grass of the earth') is fulfilled precisely — not through Eliphaz's mechanism but through Job's vindicated faithfulness.
17: wayyāmāṯ ʾiyyôḇ zāqēn ûśəḇaʿ yāmîm: 'And Job died, old and full of days.' The closing formula zāqēn ûśəḇaʿ yāmîm (old and full of days) is the patriarchal death formula: Abraham (Gen 25:8: zāqēn wəśāḇēaʿ), Isaac (Gen 35:29: zāqēn ûśəḇaʿ yāmîm), David (1 Chr 29:28: śāḇaʿ yāmîm ʿōšer wəḵāḇôḏ). Eliphaz's promise of dying 'like a sheaf gathered up in its season' (5:26) is fulfilled word for word. The book that began with Job at the height of his prosperity, stripped of everything, descending to the ash heap — ends with the same man living 140 additional years, full of days, in covenant-completed rest. The theodicy is not an argument about why Job suffered; it is a narrative demonstration that faithful suffering-under-divine-sovereignty leads to this: a death of fullness, not of abandonment. The last word of the Hebrew book is yāmîm (days) — the same word that haunted the suffering Job ('my days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle,' 7:6) is now the word of completion and satisfaction.
